_provenance.note: This Stage 1 JSON was reconstructed from the currently-published cards so external Stage 2 (Gemini bias) and Stage 3 (Codex fact-check) reviews can verify what readers see. It is not the original analysis — the original pipeline was not run on this issue.

headline: SUHAKAM Estimates Half a Million to One Million Stateless Children in Sabah
context: SUHAKAM estimates 500,000 to 1 million stateless children in Sabah, far higher than commonly cited figures. Tens of thousands are excluded from schooling. The Bajau Laut community is the most visible affected group.

cards.text: SUHAKAM: 500,000 to 1 million stateless children in Sabah, among the largest statelessness concentrations in Southeast Asia.
cards.sub: Tens of thousands are excluded from formal schooling per UNICEF. Official statistics structurally underplay the true scale.

cards.h: Federal Constitution Article 14 grants citizenship by birth under specific conditions. Stateless children fall through gaps in jus soli and jus sanguinis.
cards.s: The conditions assume documentation that the children's parents often do not have, perpetuating statelessness across generations.
cards.d: Federal Constitution Article 14 grants citizenship by birth under specific conditions. Stateless children fall through gaps in jus soli and jus sanguinis. The conditions assume documentation that the children's parents often do not have, perpetuating statelessness across generations.

cards.h: Malaysia has not ratified the 1961 Convention on the Reduction of Statelessness, leaving no treaty obligation to provide a path to citizenship.
cards.s: SUHAKAM has repeatedly recommended ratification. The recommendation has not been acted upon by successive governments.
cards.d: Malaysia has not ratified the 1961 Convention on the Reduction of Statelessness, leaving no treaty obligation to provide a path to citizenship. SUHAKAM has repeatedly recommended ratification. The recommendation has not been acted upon by successive governments.

cards.h: The Bajau Laut (Sea Gypsy) community is the most visible affected group, described by researchers as 'genuinely stateless' across Malaysia, Indonesia and the Philippines.
cards.s: Many Bajau Laut children were born in Sabah and have never set foot in another country, but no state recognises them as citizens.
cards.d: The Bajau Laut (Sea Gypsy) community is the most visible affected group, described by researchers as 'genuinely stateless' across Malaysia, Indonesia and the Philippines. Many Bajau Laut children were born in Sabah and have never set foot in another country, but no state recognises them as citizens.

cards.text: The story is not 12,000 invisible children. It is potentially a million, and the official enumeration system cannot see them at all.
Statelessness in Sabah is structural exclusion. It needs citizenship-pathway legislation and the will to count the population honestly.

confidence: Low — derived from published cards, original brief not on disk; submit for retroactive Stage 2/3 verification
